rationale_for_baseline_tier: Viral gastroenteritis is usually self-care, but this patient cannot keep fluids down and lives alone, creating a dehydration risk and a need to reassess if oral rehydration fails, so prompt review is appropriate (T2). Surgical abdomen routes to ED (T4); failed rehydration/invasive features route to ED.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Severe constant or one-sided (e.g. right-lower) abdominal pain, a rigid/very tender abdomen, or vomiting with no flatus/stool and a bloated belly
escalation_edges.rationale: Possible appendicitis / bowel obstruction — surgical emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain becomes severe and constant or localises (e.g. to your right lower tummy), your abdomen becomes rigid and very tender, or you stop passing wind/stool with a bloated belly — go to the Emergency Department promptly.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Unable to keep any fluids down despite the antiemetic, not passing urine, marked lightheadedness/faintness; or blood in the stool or vomit, or a high fever
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you can't keep any fluids down even with the anti-nausea wafer, stop passing urine, feel very faint, or notice blood in your stool or vomit or a high fever — go to the Emergency Department.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower threshold for ED if rehydration fails; give explicit dehydration and surgical red flags.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Take the anti-nausea wafer, then rehydration solution in tiny sips every few minutes, and rest. The vomiting usually settles within a day. No antibiotics or anti-diarrhoea tablets are needed.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Oral rehydration ± antiemetic + review in 48h; ED for surgical abdomen or failed rehydration
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: ED for severe/localised pain, blood in stool/vomit, or if you can't keep fluids down.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Acute viral gastroenteritis; surgical abdomen and invasive-diarrhoea features screened negative; oral rehydration + ondansetron; dehydration/surgical safety-nets given.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Attributing to gastroenteritis with no surgical/dehydration screen or safety-net.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An appendicitis is mislabelled as a tummy bug and perforates, or the patient becomes significantly dehydrated.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: No dehydration safety-net in a patient living alone who can't retain fluids.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Ambulance for a viral tummy bug that rehydrates with sips — unnecessary.
